items.rationale: He sees SNARKs as a transformative, rapidly innovating technology central to scaling blockchains and reshaping trust and privacy, and has staked his career on it by joining a16z crypto research.
items.age_note: Core thesis stated 2022-09 through 2022-11; consistently reinforced, not restated verbatim after 2022-11-17 but never contradicted.

citations.quote: This space is experiencing an explosion of innovation, and I think this technology can be transformative.
I believe blockchains will transform society's mechanisms for establishing trust & privacy

items.rationale: He believes sum-check protocols minimize total prover work due to near-perfect memory locality, and expects good hardware implementations to follow; this is his preferred technical direction over FFT/FRI-based approaches.

citations.quote: sum-check-based SNARKs remain the most promising for minimizing total prover work, and that good hardware implementations will follow.

items.rationale: He is teasing forthcoming work demonstrating the power of multivariate polynomials, signaling personal research conviction in this approach.

citations.quote: We'll see more demonstrations of the power of multivariate polynomials soon!

items.rationale: He argues circuit-avoiding, special-purpose protocols give night-and-day prover gains and won't be subsumed by recursion; he cites verifiable neural network training/inference as an imminent compelling application he is personally working on.

citations.quote: One that I think we'll see soon is neural network training/inference... I'm working on some others that I hope people will find compelling.
some critical applications are amenable to super-efficient protocols that entirely avoid circuits... night-and-day prover performance improvements.

items.rationale: He dove deep into validity rollups and was surprised by their richness; he views them as a key scalability application of SNARKs, though he treats them as amenable to (not requiring) recursion.
items.age_note: Enthusiasm expressed 2022-08; later (2022-12-31) framed more analytically around rollups' amenability to recursion.

citations.quote: I learned a lot about validity rollups and their subtleties---I was surprised by just how much there was to learn.

items.rationale: He calls recursion extremely powerful and currently pervasive for keeping circuit sizes manageable, though his excitement is hedged by security caution and a belief non-recursive techniques may ultimately win.
items.age_note: Optimism (2022-12-30) is paired with recurring security caution; net stance is cautiously positive.

citations.quote: Recursion is extremely powerful. My comment shouldn't be regarded as pessimism... optimism that non-recursive techniques will prove to be even better

items.rationale: He warns that known attacks on non-interactively run FRI at 80 bits of security are feasible today, posing a systemic risk to the rollup ecosystem securing hundreds of millions in assets; he singles out dYdX's StarkEx verifier as too low.
items.age_note: Most recent and most forceful articulation (2023-03-09), escalating a concern first raised 2022-09-14.

citations.quote: Known attacks on FRI when run non-interactively at 80 bits of security are feasible... a systemic risk to the rollup ecosystem.
the dYdX StarkEx verifier remains at 80 bits... I continue to believe that this is too low.

items.rationale: He worries the inherent tension in FRI between verifier cost/proof size and security gets resolved in favor of performance, risking eroded trust and misappropriated funds.

citations.quote: this tension may get resolved in favor of performance over security.
if we prioritize performance over security, we may cause people to lose trust... potentially see misappropriated funds

items.rationale: He cautions that all implemented composed SNARKs have heuristic security or unquantified concrete-security loss, with risks compounding at large recursion depth, so some conservative entities won't use the tech yet.

citations.quote: All implemented composed SNARKs have heuristic security or at least significant loss in concrete security can't be ruled out
recursion brings both overhead and imperfectly-understood security, especially when new SNARK-friendly hash functions are used.

items.rationale: He flags that projects deploying Poseidon at ~95 bits of security makes it premature to relax on security, given recursion leans heavily on these new, less-understood hashes.

citations.quote: while projects are deploying Poseidon with at most 95 bits of security, it's premature to relax on this.

risk_regime.rationale: Thaler writes exclusively as a cryptography researcher about SNARK design, performance, and security. He expresses no market, price, or token-allocation views and explicitly disclaims investment advice. His posture is structurally optimistic on ZK technology's long-run impact but security-cautious about current deployments — a technical, not a market, stance.

notes: Subject is a16z crypto Research Partner Justin Thaler; corpus is entirely technical (SNARK/ZK cryptography), so 'sectors' are rendered as technology directions rather than investment sectors. No tokens are discussed as investments — StarkWare/StarkNet/dYdX/StarkEx and Ethereum appear only as technical referents in a security debate, not as bullish/bearish token calls, so token arrays are empty. Most recent material (2023-03-09 to 2023-03-11) sharpens his FRI 80-bit security alarm and teases multivariate-polynomial work; these were weighted most heavily. Recurring tension across the corpus: enthusiasm for SNARK capability vs. persistent insistence on adequate concrete security.
